narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background, you've put in the effort to pursue law school, including committing to full-time studies, but I understand how disappointing it must be to receive this rejection notice. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 71% chance of failure for you, which is high enough to lead to the denial of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by a few key aspects of your academic profile that stood out as concerns when compared to typical students. Most influential was your LSAT score of 25, which is notably below the average of about 37 and strongly pushed the model toward predicting bar failure, as higher scores are common among those who pass. Next, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3—lower than the typical 5.5—further reinforced this risk, signaling weaker performance relative to peers in that later stage. On a more positive note, your Year 1 decile of 5, which aligns closely with the average of 5.7, actually worked in your favor by nudging the prediction toward success. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.4, below the usual 3.2, added some additional weight toward the failure prediction, though less strongly than the others. Being full-time, like over 90% of students, provided a small positive boost, while your middle-income bracket had minimal impact. Overall, your profile compares to typical admitted students by having strengths in early performance and study commitment but falling short in critical areas like LSAT, later grades, and undergrad GPA that the model sees as strong bar exam predictors. To move forward, I recommend retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, strengthening your later-year grades if possible, and perhaps seeking targeted prep courses or tutoring to address these gaps—many students in similar situations have succeeded by focusing here and reapplying stronger. You're clearly motivated, and with these adjustments, you can shift the odds in your favor for future opportunities. Hang in there; this is just one data point on your path.
